paragraphs.body: Dear Jamaica Kincaid, My name is Daniel Patel, and I am a student exploring the concept of nontraditional essays in my writing composition class. I recently read your essay, Girl, and found it not only fascinating, but brilliant in its lasting-impact due to the unconventional structure. The approach you used, a single and uninterrupted monologue, is unique and allowed me to understand and really empathize with the idea that society pressures women to act or be a certain way, leaving no room for complaint.

I noticed how you used italics for certain phrases, presumably the daughter’s reaction to the mother’s list of commands, specifically in the phrases “but I don’t sing benna on Sundays at all and never in Sunday school” and “but what if the baker won’t let me feel the bread?” (Kincaid 320-321). These lines really spoke to me because there wasn’t much resistance from the daughter.

Like you too, I grew up with a very traditional view on how women should act in order to be proper, as my parents are both immigrants from India. I have seen firsthand how these expectations can be passed down throughout the generations, often without any question or resistance. Your essay made me really reflect on the ways cultural and familial expectations can shape identity, and how there is often little complaint to these methods

annotations.text: grew up with a very traditional view on how women should act in order to be proper, as my parents are both immigrants from India. I have seen firsthand how these expectations can be passed down throughout the generations, often without any question or resistan
annotations.comment: I would really love to see you explore this further--what connections can you make between your own upbringing and Kincaid's?

paragraphs.body: I also have a few questions about your approach. Why did you choose to write in such an assertive and dominant tone from a mother’s perspective? Was the lack of resistance from the daughter a symbol of how they often don’t have much input in these decisions and are always simply told what to do–just as they are always simply expected to follow?

annotations.text: y did you choose to write in such an assertive and dominant tone from a mother’s perspective? Was the lack of resistance from the daughter a symbol of how they often don’t have much input in these decisions and are always simply told what to do–just as they are always simply expected to follo
annotations.comment: These are great questions! I would love to know Kincaid's answers to them as well!

paragraphs.body: Thank you for writing this essay that not only showcases how cruel society can be towards women, but challenges the reader to think about it differently. Your work has inspired me to explore different methods in my writing.

annotations.comment: You're doing a great job of engaging this work and incorporating your own experiences and observations into the writing. Your questions are really compelling. They also got me thinking--it's been nearly 50 years since Kincaid wrote this--I wonder if these lessons continue to impact her in the same way and whether she has passed them on to others. Anyway, looking forward to seeing what you do next.
